text: The laparoscopic view shows a large, thick-walled duplication cyst occupying the right hypochondrium of the three-year-old patient. The camera port is positioned in the suprapubic area, providing a clear view of the abdominal lump.

The surgeon places instrument ports and aspirates the cystic structure before beginning the de-roofing process. A vessel sealing device is used to ensure precise dissection and minimize bleeding during the near-total excision of the cyst.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to carefully dissect around the duplication cyst, aiming to preserve as much of the mesentery as possible during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the roof of the cyst away from the mesentery, with visible precision to preserve as much of the mesentery as possible. The cyst is located in the right hypochondrium, and the dissection aims to minimize mesenteric damage while considering subtotal excision.

The surgeon completes the near-total excision of the duplication cyst, with the mesenteric vessels visible at the bottom of the surgical field. The surgeon examines a globular structure that remains after the excision.

The surgeon identifies a solid structure embedded in the mesentery, which is later determined to be a malformed infected node. The surgeon excises this structure, carefully enucleating it without damaging surrounding tissues. Two such structures are removed, one small and taken out with the cyst roof, and the second larger one excised separately.

After removing the cyst, the surgical field is thoroughly washed, revealing a section of the mesentery that has been excised. A portion of the bowel appears devascularized, lacking visible blood supply, prompting the decision to excise this segment of the bowel.
The surgeon prepares for an end-to-end anastomosis of the ileum, ensuring proper orientation of the mesentery for optimal alignment during the laparoscopic small bowel excision and anastomosis.

The surgeon checks the orientation of the mesentery to ensure the bowel is not twisted before proceeding with the excision. A vessel sealing device is used to excise approximately six inches of bowel, minimizing bleeding from the vascular structures during the resection.

The surgeon observes the bowel tissue, noting areas that appear dusky and congested. Using a vessel sealing device, they excise the unhealthy sections of the bowel, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a ligature to cut the margin and open the distal lumen of the bowel, revealing the interior of the bowel lumen during the laparoscopic small bowel resection.

The surgeon places a hitching stitch on the medial end of the bowel, using an outside-in and inside-out technique. This cornel stitch is tied off to provide support for the anastomosis, with a small amount of bleeding visible during the process.

The surgeon confirms the alignment of the bowel ends medially before proceeding with the anastomosis using a cornel stitch. The Maryland forceps used in the procedure appear different from standard Maryland forceps.
The surgeon begins suturing the posterior layer of the bowel anastomosis using a continuous technique, ensuring inclusion of the serosa and aiming to minimize mucosal involvement.

The surgeon applies continuous interlocking sutures to the bowel, ensuring each knot is securely locked to prevent laxity in the suture line. The technique involves taking minimal mucosa while maximizing cirrhosa inclusion, maintaining luminal integrity during the small bowel resection.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the bowel anastomosis, leaving the interlocked sutures long without tying them off. A fresh suture material, four OPDs, is prepared to begin the anterior layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon begins suturing the anterior layer of the small bowel anastomosis using a separate suture material, ensuring that the suture lines run independently from the posterior layer. This technique prevents constriction of the bowel lumen by avoiding encircling the entire structure with a single suture.

The surgeon uses a continuous suture technique to invaginate the mucosa into the lumen, minimizing mucosal bulk to prevent pouting. The suture passes from the mucosa to the cirrhosa, aiming to reduce mucosal inclusion while maintaining luminal integrity.

The surgeon applies a second layer of sutures over the initial layer in the small bowel anastomosis, focusing on the jejunum or ileum. This two-layer technique is commonly used in small bowel procedures to ensure secure anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis, with multiple threads visible in the surgical field. The medial corneling stitch is seen with a long end, alongside the distal posterior layer thread and the anterior layer thread being run through. Careful attention is required to avoid entangling these threads during the procedure.

The surgeon approaches the lateral edge of the small bowel anastomosis, preparing to tie a knot to connect the anterior and posterior suture lines. Both threads with needles are visible inside the abdomen, and the surgeon ties the first knot before cutting off the needle from one thread and removing it.

The surgeon uses the left hand to wrap the suture around the right hand or needle holder, initiating the second layer of interrupted sutures. The stitches are zero muscular, aiming to bury the mucosa and avoid cutting through the tissue. Excess threads are trimmed to prevent clutter, facilitating the completion of the anastomosis.
The completed anastomosis of the small bowel is visible. The surgeon proceeds to close the mesenteric gap using sutures.
The surgeon completes the closure of the mesentery defect following the excision of the bowel, with part of the cyst removed along with the excised bowel.
